speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 27th, 1996. We are interviewing Ann Eichner, E-I-C-H-N-E-R in Coconut Creek, Florida, and this is Tape 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. Eichner, we ended with them taking away the SS girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All right. We all were relieved that we were going to be saved. Not that it was such a happy occasion, because we didn't have anybody left. My mother died. I didn't know when my father died-- where, how. My brother, nothing. But then I was so weak I couldn't even walk. A few days later came an announcement that a Swedish man-- a, a Swedish man from the Red Cross-- is going to take a lot of people to Sweden. Before that, they took us, mostly sick people, to a rundhaus, they call it, in German. There were officers quarters which were empty already. The officers flew. They ran. And they took us on stretchers to the outskirts of Bergen-Belsen. In other words, Bergen-Belsen was the outskirts, but Bergen was the town. I was among them. They fed us. We finally got some food in us, and they made sure that we would be a little bit more healthy. Then the order came that the Swedish king, he said-- he agreed he's going to send people to take us to Sweden for convalescent so they can heal us. I don't know what happened with the other people, a lot of them. The more healthier people. They were going each way, each other way. I don't know if they found somebody or not. They were running, because the war was over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from there, his name was [? Bernardat, ?] a Swedish man. He came from the direction of the king, King Gustaf in Sweden, and made arrangements for us to go with a boat to Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken on stretchers to the boat. I remember like today. And we wound up in Uddevalla, it was called-- a town. Uddevalla in Sweden. They took us to an empty school house, and they had doctors there. And little by little, they fed us. The worst part of it was, on the street it in Sweden, in Uddevalla, there were a lot of people, Swedish people, looking up. We should come to the window. They wanted to see the victims. We all were without hair. And a lot of girls didn't want-- boys, too, they didn't want to go to the window. But they were very kind. The king sent us presents and sent us food. And the doctors were there. And that was-- they made sure we had hot showers so we don't bring the disease to Sweden. Hot and cold. From hot to cold. They were very nice to us, the Swedish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, we got well. And in the hospital-- I happened to have an appendix operation. I had pain in my appendix. And the last minute, the doctor came in and he said-- I didn't understand Swedish, but I understand. He told me, operation. Operation? I knew I had to have an operation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't listen to me. I didn't want to go. They took me, and they operated on me. And when I got well, they sent us to a camp for healthy people so we could adjust to the country and take care of ourselves. We were there in that camp quite awhile, until we got well enough. And they selected people to go all different places. Me they took with other girls to a school. I was young yet. And they took me to a school-- the religious school, it happened to be. And that's where I got the pictures from.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could have been like 15? I don't really remember. It has to be-- I was 13 when the war broke out. That was 1944 when they took us. From '39, how old could I have been?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: 26. It was 1944. So you were 18.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been 18. Because we were-- right. I must have been 18. So they took us. They taught us a little-- you know, how to be a people again. Because we were scared. We saw some-- or whatever. We couldn't adjust so well, and also health-wise. Then they selected people to go-- to learn how to support ourselves. To learn so we could function. Me with other girls they sent to-- from that school, they sent a lot of girls who were volunteering to go to Israel. They send them to Israel. I was a few girls who couldn't go, because there wasn't enough place. I was among them. They took me to Goteborg. Get That's the second biggest town in Sweden. They took me to Goteborg, and they taught me how to sew on a machine. And I was there until I don't know how long. Then I could work. They send me to Axvall. Axvall was a-- I'm talking German already. There was a place for tuberculosis. How do you call the--

speaker: interviewer
text: A sanatorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sanatorium, right. To work there as a help, a nurse's help. I was there a few weeks, and they realized I'm too young. I could catch tuberculosis. So they sent me back to Goteborg. In Goteborg, I worked-- called a [NON-ENGLISH]. In a school where they had invalid kids, I mean, sick kids. Not sick physically, but mentally. It was like a school to, to-- a rehabilitation school. I was there awhile, and I was already well. Well enough to walk around, to go places. I had girlfriends. I made boyfriends. I worked awhile there. And from there, I met another Holocaust survivor, a guy. And we got friendly. We started being together. From the same town as I was born. He had the same experiences. So we got close with each other. And eventually, we married. We married in 1951, I think. I had it here. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We married, and we both worked in the same factory, sewing factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, we tried to make our life back. But all the memories in our families, we could never find anybody. He couldn't find anybody. I couldn't find anybody. Not my brother. I don't know where he disappeared. There were papers, English papers, all around the world papers. They asked us to write to them, and they going to look for-- for our siblings, for parents. I wrote, and I wrote. Didn't get no answer. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it 1951? I, I was writing to my aunt here in America. She was-- she went-- before they sent everybody to get to us, she somehow was smuggled to America, to the. United States. She got in touch with me. I knew I had an aunt in America, so I wrote to the Jewish organization, JOINT, to look for her. And they found her. And eventually, she sent papers for me to emigrate to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took quite a while. I was from Goteborg. We went to live in Falkoping. And Boras-- Boras in Sweden, where we both were very happy. We still were young. And we married. And he couldn't have no papers, because my aunt could only send it for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So 19-- wait a minute. I have it written down what time I came to the United States. It was 1950-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] '58 or something-- oh, no. What am I talking about? 1951. 28th of December, my papers were ready, and I went on a ship, Gripsholm, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Goteborg to go to the United States. I left my husband behind. And I promised to work on it, he should come to United States also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which I did. I arrived in the United States, and the organization-- the Joint it was called-- in New York. It was a big, big, big thing to me to come to a county like this. I didn't know the language. I didn't-- well, in Sweden, I did learn a little English. Because I wanted to. I knew I'm going to come to the United States. And I happen to be very talented in languages. And I comprehended a little. So just to get me by. I came to the United States, and I was very lonely. And a Jewish organizations found some people for me which I knew from the same town in Sweden, Boras. Which they immigrated, also. There were-- they were here in the United States, and she got me together with them. Because I had my aunt. And I went to Ellis Island many times myself to argue with the authorities to let my husband come to the United States. It took about, I think, a year. And I moved at that time to Paterson, New Jersey from New York. I worked in a factory. They call it sweat shops. I was in one. I had to support myself. And I went to pick him up. That time, I had already friends, and I was halfway happy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he came here to the United States and started to go to school. He was my age. He was born in July, the same year, and I was born in December. And he made eventually the electric license. He was an electrician. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we decided to have a family, and the rest is probably history. I had two kids, beautiful children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a housewife, he was working. And we made a life in the United States. But I never found any of my relatives-- any of my mother or-- mother was dead. Father, I didn't know where he died. I looked for my brother high and wide, and I never found anything about him. I don't know how I survived, but I must have been God's will that I should make a new generation. That's the only thing I could figure out. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's what it is. And I belong to the organizations, you know, Holocaust survivors. And we're all like a family, because we don't have a family. It's not easy. But thank God, United States is a wonderful country. We made a living. My kids grew up. They married. They have children. And I'm going to be 69, and I'm going to live to see my grandson's bar mitzvah, my first one. I never thought, being in Auschwitz, that I ever come to that. It's probably God's will.

speaker: interviewee
text: But from all these things, they took everything away from our lives. And just because we are Jewish. [DRINKS] I just hope to God [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that it never repeats itself. Because really thinking about it, who could understand what we went through? When they tell me about all those nations, in Yugoslavia and all over, I am sympathizing fine. But it's still not the same. You can't. And so many times, if I talk to the American people, they-- some of them in the beginning said, what do you mean you didn't have breads? Or you could eat potatoes. I look at them. There's no answer. Who had potatoes? So I just hope to God that that never occurs again, that my grandchildren will be happy and, and, and, and not have that kind of a world. Mrs. Eichner, introduce that handsome gentleman to your side.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Fred Eichner, which I met a nice few years after my first husband went, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went away from me. My kids were all married. I had grandchildren. We come from the same county, and a lot of things bind us together. He comes from Krakow, Poland. And we got together. And finally we decided we're going to emigrate to Florida from New Jersey and made a home for ourselves. He was-- before the war, he was a boxer. Not a professional, there was no professionals. Amateur boxer. He had a lot of friends. And he seems to be very happy in Florida. So that's what we are here, from 1982. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I hope we'll be well enough to be many years together. Catch That's all I can tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something, Mr. Eichner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, I met my wife. She was young and beautiful. This what we decide, you know, be together. We're both continuing over here in the [? winter. ?] And we stay together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you have to mention that his wife died about eight years ago before we met.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he has children, which are my children also. We have a big family right now. And I try to be happy not to remember all that which can never happen, and we just both hope that this is enough. The world had enough. I hope it never repeats itself. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a picture taken 1939, summertime. It's the year when the war broke out. But before, my aunt-- this is my father, Pinchas, and his sister, my aunt [INAUDIBLE], Bronia. And she was in a resort in Poland named Szvzvnica, S-Z-C-Z-V-N-I-C-A. And my father was visiting his sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me in 1951. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In Paterson, New Jersey, when I came to the United States. This picture is taken in Sweden, 1945, when I still didn't have much hair. It didn't grow back. And this was my girlfriend. In the city of Falkoping. This picture was taken in Sweden 1945 or '46. And at [INAUDIBLE] park. That what was called in Swedish. And two of my girlfriends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in the beginning of 1945. After the quarantine, I was in Sweden, in Uddevalla. They sent us to a school, to a school with all other girls which they were from all kind of camps-- not just from Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in 1980 in Wayne, New Jersey. On the right hand side is my son and his wife, my daughter and her husband, my grandson, Simon, my granddaughter, Katie, me, Matthew and Brian next.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my newest addition of grandchildren. She is four years old, and her name is Sydney. Sydney Krantz, my daughter's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me and my husband. It's our wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?
